headline: GRAINS EXTEND SELLING, HOGS CRATER 6%
subheadline: Memorial Day liquidation continues as funds book profits into long weekend.
lead: Corn closed $4.47, down nine cents for the week's biggest single-day drop. The funds that drove May's rally are stepping out ahead of June planting deadlines, and Friday's technical selling confirmed what Thursday's profit-taking started. Hogs dropped 6% to $95.85 as packers pushed back on Memorial Day premium, turning what looked like seasonal strength into outright liquidation.
the_takeaway: The Memorial Day money is out; June data decides what's next.
teaser: Pre-holiday liquidation turns into weekly rout across grain complex.

one_number.unit: corn's weekly drop, steepest since March
one_number.context: Technical selling accelerated Friday as July corn lost the $4.50 support that held all month. Fund positioning that looked defensive Tuesday turned into outright exit strategy by week's end. The move confirms what basis has been saying: elevator demand isn't keeping pace with the rally momentum.

sections.title: Grains Extend Memorial Rout
sections.body: Corn dropped nine cents Friday to $4.47, the week's steepest daily decline and confirmation that **Memorial Day liquidation turned systemic**. Soybeans fell twelve cents to $11.87 as technical selling accelerated into the close. Wheat joined the selloff, down eleven cents to $6.11. The funds that drove May's rally are booking profits ahead of June planting data, and Friday's action showed what happens when profit-taking meets thin holiday liquidity. China's continued absence from U.S. soy markets added pressure, with unknown destinations the only buyer in Friday's export sales.
sections.catalyst: Technical selling accelerated as corn broke $4.50 support level
sections.bottom_line: Memorial Day money is rotating out faster than seasonal patterns suggested.
sections.vs_yesterday: Yesterday's profit-taking became Friday's technical breakdown.

sections.title: Hogs Crash Into Holiday
sections.body: Lean hogs crashed 6% to $95.85, the contract's biggest single-day drop since February as **packers refused to pay Memorial Day premium**. Live cattle bucked the trend, gaining 2.9% to $248.25, but feeders fell 1.3% to $348.42 as the convergence trade that worked all month finally broke. The hog crash came despite tight supplies, suggesting packer pushback on seasonal pricing rather than fundamental weakness. Cattle's divergence from the broader liquidation suggests the live market found its buyer, but feeder weakness points to cost-of-gain concerns.
sections.catalyst: Packers rejected Memorial Day premium in direct cash cattle trade
sections.bottom_line: Cattle holding divergent strength while hog premium collapses.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC Hears Fertilizer Price Complaints From Iowa Farmers
outside_the_pit.body: Eastern Iowa producer Lance Lillibridge told Federal Trade Commission officials that current fertilizer prices are unsustainable, saying farmers are going broke. The hearing signals potential regulatory attention to input costs that haven't retreated despite lower natural gas.

outside_the_pit.title: Theileria Parasite Detected in Nebraska Cattle Herds
outside_the_pit.body: Asian Longhorned Tick-carried Theileria parasites found in several Nebraska counties, prompting vigilance warnings. The tick-borne disease affects cattle health and could complicate herd management heading into summer pasture season.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Specialty Crop Assistance Opens Monday
outside_the_pit.body: The agency will distribute $1.625 billion in payments starting June 1 for eligible producers affected by weather disasters. Payment rates are set, marking the largest specialty crop assistance program in recent memory.

the_more_you_know.title: Why basis narrows when futures crater
the_more_you_know.body: Corn basis tightened this week even as July futures dropped 28 cents from Tuesday's high. When elevators see futures selling off, they know producers will hold grain longer, hoping for a bounce. That expected supply tightening makes local buyers compete harder for available bushels. The result: basis firms up exactly when futures weaken, creating the margin expansion that keeps elevators profitable through volatile periods. It's counterintuitive but reliable: the bigger the futures selloff, the more basis has to work to pull grain out of storage.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn planting above 85% confirms Belt is on schedule despite May weather concerns.
Weekly export sales: soy under 200K MT keeps China absent from U.S. markets.
June acreage intentions data: any corn reduction below 90.8M acres adds weather premium back.

daily_quote.text: An economist's guess is liable to be just as good as anybody else's.

source_summary: CBOT, CME, EIA, USDA, news wires
